This is in response to your letter dated May 5, 1980, 
Canflict af Interest 
322-6444 
on behalf of Councilman Fred Barrera regarding the disquali-
fication provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
The following 
advice is provided pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b). 
The facts as I understand them from you and the City Planning 
Director are as follows. 
The J & L Oil Company (hereinafter referred to as J & L) 
operated a gasoline station in the City of Orange. 
In February 
of 1980 J & L filed an application for a conditional use 
permit to dispense beer and wine and to operate a convenience 
store at the station. 
In order to sell beer and wine, the 
station would be limited to self-service pumps. 
The application submitted by J & L was heard and approved 
by the Zoning Administrator March 20, 1980. 
Residents in 
the area who were opposed to the application asked Councilman 
Barrera to appeal the Administrator's decision to the City 
Council so they could avoid payment of a $50 appeals fee 
which would have been required if they had instituted the 
appeal themselves. 
Councilman Barrera did appeal the granting 
of the conditional use permit March 28, 1980, and the matter 
is now pending before the City Council. 
Councilman Barrera owns a gasoline station at the corner 
of Chapman and Batavia Streets, approximately one mile from 
the J & L station. 
Seventy-five percent of the gross income 
of Councilman Barrera's station results from his service and 
repair business, and the remaining 25 percent results from 
the sale of gasoline and oil products. 
His station pumps 
approximately 30,000 gallons of'<]asoline a month, but this 
figure varies by as much as 6 to 8 thousand gallons from 
month-to-month. 
Councilman Barrera's station is closed on 
Saturdays, Sundays and weekday evenings after 6:00 p.m. 


· ) 
Furman A. Roberts 
June 3, 1980 
Page Two 
Councilman Barrera's station is located on Chapman 
Avenue, one of the city's major commercial streets, approximately 
one-half mile west of Glassell Street. 
The J & L station is 
located on the corner of Glassell and Walnut Avenue, approximately 
one-half mile north of Chapman and is not visible from Councilman 
Barrera's station. 
The Department of Energy has determined 
that for gasoline allocation purposes the market area for a 
service station in an urban area (population exceeds 25,000) 
is a radius of one mile from the station. 
There are approximately 
eight stations, including the J & L station, located in 
Councilman Barrera's market area. 
Of these eight stations, 
two currently operate convenience stores that sell beer and 
wine, and one of these is located on Chapman, less than a 
mile from Councilman Barrera's station. 
If J & L is granted 
a conditional use permit to sell beer and wine, it would, 
therefore, be the third such facility located within the 
market area of Councilman Barrera's gas station. 
There are 
two convenience markets selling beer, wine and liquor directly 
across the street £rma Councilman Barrera's station. 
In 
addition, across the street from the J & L station there are 
a liquor store and a small market selling beer and wine in 
addition to groceries. 
You have asked whether Councilman Barrera may participate 
in the City Council's discussion and decision regarding the 
appeal. 
Government Code Section 87100 provides that: 
No public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any 
way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason 
to know he has a financial interest. 
Expanding this concept, Government Code Section 87103 
states in part that: 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one 
thousand dollars ($1,000)~ 


Furman A. Roberts 
June 3, 1980 
Page Three 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institution in the regular course of 
business on terms available to the public without regard 
to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars 
($250) or more in value provided to, received by or 
promised to the public official within twelve months 
prior to the time when the decision is made; •••• 
Although not specifically provided in the facts presented 
to our office it is reasonable to assume that the value of 
Councilman Barrera's investment in his gas station exceeds 
one thousand dollars and that the station has been a source 
of income to him in excess of $250 during the preceding 
twelve months. 
Therefore, Councilman Barrera is required by 
Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 to disqualify himself 
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the granting of a condi-
tional use permit to J & L Oil Company will foreseeably have 
a material financial effect on his gas station. 
The Commission has provided by regulation that: 
(a) 
The financial effect of a governmental decision 
on a financial interest of a public official is material 
if the decision will have a significant effect on the 
business entity, real property or source of income in 
question. 
(b) 
In determining whether it is reasonably fore-
seeable that the effects of a governmental decision 
will be significant within the meaning of the general 
standard set forth in paragraph (a), consideration 
should be given to the following factors: 
(1) 
Whether, in the case of a business entity 
in which the public official holds a direct or 
indirect investment of one thousand dollars ($1,000) 
or more or in the case of a business entity in 
which tne public official is a director, officer, 
partner, employee, trustee or holds any position 
of management, the effect of the decision will be 
to increase or decrease: 
(A) 
The annualized gross revenues by 
the lesser of: 
1. 
One hundred thousand dollars 
($100,000); or 


Furman A. Roberts 
June 3, 1980 
Page Four 
2. 
One percent if it is one thousand 
dollars ($1,000) or more; or 
(B) 
Annual net income by the lesser of: 
1. 
Fifty thousand dollars ($50,000); 
or 
~ 
One half of one percent if it 
is one thousand dollars ($1,000) or 
more; or 
(C) 
Current assets or liabilities by 
the lesser of: 
1. 
One hundred thousand dollars 
($100,000); or 
2. 
One half of one percent if it 
is one thousand dollars ($1,000) or 
more. 
Current assets are deemed to be 
decreased by the amount of any expenses 
incurred as a result of a governmental 
decision. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code §18702.l1 
The Commission has provided guidance by formal written 
opinion for determining when a financial effect is reasonably 
foreseeable. 
Thorner, 1 FPPC Opinions 198 (No. 75-089, 
Dec. 4, 1975). 
In Thorner, the Commission noted that deter-
minations of foreseeability must be made on a case-by-case 
basis. 
The mere fact that an effect is conceivable is not 
sufficient to make the effect "foreseeable." 
On the other 
hand, it is not necessary to determine that the effect will 
in fact occur. 
The ultimate test is whether or not there is 
a substantial probability or likelihood that there will be a 
material effect. 
Based on the facts as you have presented them to us, it 
is reasonable to assume that any competitive effects of the 
addition of a convenience store operation at the J & L station 
!I This section of the regulation also applies to 
business entities which are sources of income within the 
meaning of Government Code Section 87103(c). 


, ,

will be spread among all the service stations, convenience 
stores and liquor stores in the area. 
We cannot conclude that there is a substantial probability 
or a likelihood that Councilman Barrera's business will be 
affected in a material manner by a decision to allow J & L 
to sell beer and wine or any other items at its station on a 
different street approximately one mile away. 
Consequently, 
the Political Reform Act does not require that he disqualify 
himself from participating in the City Council's consid27ation 
of the appeal from the Zoning Administrator's decision.-
Please note that under the provisions of Government 
Code Section 83114(b), this written advice can serve as a 
complete defense in any enforcement proceeding initiated by 
the Fair Political Practices Commission and as evidence of 
good faith conduct an any other civil or criminal proceeding 
provided that the advice was sought in good faith and that 
all material facts have been truthfully disclosed. 
Conse-
quently, if facts do come to light which suggest that there 
is a substantial likelihood that Councilman Barrera's business 
will be affected materially this advice would no longer 
constitute a defense. 
If you have any further questions concerning this matter, 
please do not hesitate to contact me again.

We represent J & L Oil Company, a California corporation, who is 
the applicant for the conditional use permit referred to in the letter to 
you dated May 5, 1980, from Furman B. Roberts, City Attorney for the 
City of Orange. Although we have received a copy of that letter, we have 
not received copies of its enclosures. 
We, too, are interested in your opinion as to the questions propounded by 
Mr. Roberts. Additionally, we wonder whether Councilman Fred Barrera 
should even have participated in the enactment of urgency ordinance 
number 52-79 or regular ordinance number 69-79. We dislike arguing the 
facts in a letter such as this. However, in view of the obviously biased 
comments of Mr. Roberts, we cannot refrain from doing so even though 
our understanding of the facts is limited at this time. 
We understand that Mr. Barrera owns a gasoline service station which is 
less than one mile away from the gasoline service station operated by 
J & L. Although we do not know the gross income received by Mr. Barrera 
from his service business, we believe that he receives a gross profit of not 
less than 16¢ per gallon for gasoline sold by him. If he pumps an average 
of 30,000 gallons per month, as Mr. Roberts states, he is receiving gross 
profit from gasoline sales of at least $4, 800 per month. We suggest that 
his service station hours of operation are controlled to Some degree by the 
level of his competition. 
The business premises of J & L was indeed formerly used as a Gulf Oil 
Co. gasoline service station. Mr. Roberts states that it ceased business 
"several years ago, " but we understand that it was in operation until 
September, 1979. approximately one week before J & L took possession. 
Mr .. Roberts' statement that the protest to the issuance to J & L of a beer 
and "vine license was based on objections of neighborhood residents is 
disputed. 
J & L obtained signatures of some 1100 persons in favor of the 
conditional use permit with approximately 155 of the signatures being those 


p 
, 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
May 13, 1980 
Page Two 
of persons residing within a few blocks of the J & L premises. 
Our client denies that it is offering gasoline as a leader to patronize its 
market. The sale of gasoline is its principal business at that location. 
We are at a loss to explain Mr. Roberts' statement that he was told by 
"one of the sons of the applicant" (the applicant is a corporation) that 
gasoline sales are only a leader to encourage convenience market sales 
since the president of the corporation tells us that no such representation 
was made. Further, we question the propriety of Mr. Roberts' communi-
cation with our client or its representative without our consent in view of the 
provisions of Rule 7-103 of the State Bar Rules of Professional Conduct. 
J & L Oil Co. feels that the issuance of an off-sale beer and wine license 
to it will substantially increase its gasoline sales, probably by as much as 
100, 000 gallons per month. If so, it will be selling gasoline to persons who 
would otherwise have purchased elsewhere. In view thereof, it appears 
entirely reasonable to conclude that gasoline sales by Mr. Barrera will be 
significantly affected thereby. 
Thank you for your consideration of our position. 
Very truly yours, 
STUMP, GARNER & WRIGHT

In answer to the letter of Mr. William T. Garner dated 
May 13, 1980, a copy of which is attached hereto, to your 
Commission, I only wish to point out that my communication 
with Mr. Garner's client or his representative came about as 
a result of my presence at the proposed site for the purpose 
of taking polaroid photographs. 
As I was photographing the 
site and the surrounding area, I was approached by what 
appeared to be an employee at the premises and invited to 
come and speak to a gentlemen who later identified himself 
as the son of the owner. 
The person seemed eager to volun-
teer information concerning the nature and method of business 
to be conducted at the location. 
This speaker also recognized

City Council meeting the evening prior to my visit to photo-

Thereupon, I was shown certain building plans and given 
an explanation of the type of business operation which was 
contemplated to be conducted at the site. 
Any discussion 
was initiated solely by this gentleman and not be any action 
on my part. 
Therefore, far from initiating any conversation 
with the representative of Mr. Garner's client, I was, quite 
literally, propelled into conversation through the initiative 
of Mr. Garner's client's representative. 
I suppose if I had 
refused to talk to this representative, Mr. Garner would then 
accuse me of bias and refusal to listen to his applicant's 
viewpoint. 
The life of a public servant is indeed a hard 
one.

It is certainly true that the applicant presented 
petitions purporting to favor the application. 
However, it 
is also true that neighborhood residents did appear in 
opposition to the application at the hearing before the 
Zoning Administrator. 
Certainly the statements which I have attributed to 
this representative are correct to the best of my recol-
lection. 
Of course, I do not know exactly when the gasoline 
station operation ceased business. 
Perhaps there was some 
gasoline sales as late as September 1979, approximately one 
week before J & L Oil took possession. 
However, as I 
recall my observation of the premises as I traveled by on 
the street, the Gulf Oil sign was removed from the premises 
perhaps several years prior to this incident. 
At least this 
is my best recollection of this situation. 
Whether the 
station may have operated as some type of independant gas 
distributorship after the removal of the Gulf sign I do not 
know. 
If you have any further questions, please feel free to 
contact me.

Dear Ms. Cameron: 
Pursuant to our telephone conversation of Thursday, 
April 24, 1980, I am sending you this request for an opinion 
on the following questions: 
1. 
Without violating Section 87100-87103 of the 
Government Code, commonly known as the Conflict of 
Interest provisions, can Councilman Fred Barrera 
of the City Council of the City of Orange appeal 
to the City Council the granting of a Conditional 
Use Permit by the Zoning Administrator of the City 
of Orange to permit the off-sale salevof beer and 
wine at a premises devoted to a convenience market 
and to the dispensing of gasoline by self-service 
methods? 
2. 
After his appeal of the matter to the City Council, 
can City Councilman Fred Barrera legally parti-
cipate in the discussion and the decision of the 
Council on the appeal of the question of whether 
to uphold or overturn the Zoning Administrator's 
decision to grant the Conditional Use Permit? 
As I explained to you in our telephone conversation. 
Councilman Fred Barrera was recently reelected on April 
8, 
1980 to a second term on the City Council. 
~r. Barrera was 
born in the City of Orange and has, for many years, owned 
and operated a certain Union Oil Service Station located on 
the northwest corner of Batavia Street and Chapman Avenue in 
the City of Orange. 
(See photograph No. lA & IB and the No. 
1 on the City Street map upon which is marked his business 
location) 
STATUS OF COUNCILMAN BARRERA ANJ HIS 
INESS 
Mr. Barrera has been at the aforementioned location for 
over 30 years operating a general service station dispensing

Union Oil products. 
At least 75% of his gross income results 
from his service business. 
Basically, he has a large 
number of steady customers for which he performs mechanical 
and maintenance services on their vehicles. 
Perhaps 25% of 
his gross income results from the sale of gasoline and oil. 
Probably, he may pump not more than an average of 30,000 
gallons per month. 
Usually, the gallonage pumped will not 
vary by more than 6,000 or 8,000 gallons from one month to 
another. 
The amount of gasoline gallonage which he pumped 
declined some years ago when the numerous freeways surrounding 
the City of Orange were constructed. 
In fact, he closes his 
automobile service station on Saturdays, Sundays and weekday 
evenings after 6 p.m. 
Enclosed herein you will find a street map of the City 
of Orange containing a scale in feet indicating one mile and 
portions thereof. 
Also, enclosed herein you will find a 
series of numbered photographs. 
On the map is drawn a 
number of points identifying Councilman Barrera's location, 
as well as the facility proposed for dispensing gasoline and 
conducting 
a convenience market selling beer and wine. 
Also indicated on the map is the approximate straight line, 
as well as the street, distance between the two points. 
I 
have identified a number of other points on the map and I 
have identified most of the points shown on the map with the 
accompanying polaroid photographs which are also enclosed 
herein. 
You will notice that I have numbered the photographs 
and I have identified the points on the map using these 
numbers. 
The map also indicates the freeways surrounding 
the City and the distance both from Mr. Barrera's service 
station as well as the proposed service station distance to 
the nearest freeway access point. 
Also, indicated on the 
map are other self-service gasoline dealerships in the 
vicinity of Mr. Barrera's station. 
BACKGROUND FACTS 
The proposed premises are located at the southwest 
corner of Glassell and Walnut in the City of Orange (See Map 
Location No.2 & Photographs No. 2A & 2B). 
Formerly, this 
site was occupied by a Gulf Oil Station which ceased business 
operation several years ago. 
Several months ago, in August 
of 1979, the applicant, J and L Oil Company, first applied 
for a license to dispense beer and wine on an off-sale basis 
and conduct a convenience market dispensing of gasoline by 
self-service at the proposed location. No merchandise would 
be delivered to anyone in a vehicle and everyone would have 
to exit from the vehicle in order to supply his vehicle with 
gasoline and to purchase any merchandise, including beer and 
wine, within the interior of the building. 
The City Council 
authorized our office to transmit a protest to this application 
to the Alcoholic Beverage Control Department. 
This protest 
was based on the objection of the residents in the neighborhood 


May 5, 1980 
Page 3 
inasmuch as they felt that there were too many liquor stores 
and markets selling beer and wine in the vicinity. 
A copy 
of this letter of protest to the Alcoholic Beverage Control 
dated September 19, 1979, is enclosed herein. 
The minutes of the City Council of September 18, 1979, 
which evidence the decision of the Council to file the 
protest are also enclosed herein. 
At the conclusion of this 
protest hearing on September 18, 1979, the City Attorney was 
instructed to prepare an urgency ordinance pursuant to the 
authority of Section 65856 of the Government Code which 
urgency ordinance was thereupon adopted and became immediately 
effective on September 18, 1979 with its period of effectiveness 
extending to January 18, 1980. 
A copy of this Urgency 
Ordinance 52-79 is enclosed herein. 
Prior to the expiration 
of the urgency ordinance, the Planning staff was instructed 
to present to the Planning Commission a proposed amendment 
to the C-l Zone (which was the zone applicable to the proposed 
premises) amending the requirements of that zone insofar as 
the sale of alcoholic beverages at a service station site 
were concerned. 
The ultimate ordinance amending the C-l 
Zone provided that to sell alcoholic beverages on an off-
sale basis at a service station site required the approval 
of a Conditional Use Permit. 
Upon the enactment on February 26, 1980 of this permanent 
Ordinance No. 69-79, a copy of which is enclosed herein, the 
applicant, J and L Oil Company, filed an application for 
Conditional Use Permit No. 1023 to permit the off-sale sale 
of beer and wine in conjunction with a convenience market 
dispensing self service gasoline at the subject location. 
Pursuant to Ordinance No. 69-79, the Zoning Administrator 
was required to hear this application initially. 
On March 
20, 1980, Mr. John Lane, the Zoning Administrator, heard the 
Conditional Use Permit application in a public hearing and 
determined to grant the requested conditional use permit 
subject to the conditions listed in his decision. 
A copy of 
the Zoning Administrator's decision is enclosed herein. 
A number of residents in the area were opposed to the 
decision of the Zoning Administrator and prevailed upon 
Councilman Fred Barrera to appeal the granting of the 
conditional use permit. 
Councilman Barrera did appeal the 
granting of the conditional use permit on March 28, 1980. 
In accordance with Section 17.94.110 of the Orange Municipal 
Code (see attached) any interested party, member of the 
Planning Commission or City Council can appeal a decision of 
the Zoning Administrator. 
By virtue of the fact that the 
councilmen appealed the decision, the residents saved the 
$50 appeal fee normally required to appeal such decision. 
Of course, as you can see throughout the entire history 
of this proposed use, Councilman Barrera has participated in

In addition, of course, Mr.

before the Council on Tuesday, April 22, 1980. 
At the time 
of the Council hearing on April 22, 1980, the enclosed 
letter was received from the attorney for the applicant, J 
and L Oil Company, objecting to the fact that Mr. Barrera 
had in any manner participated in this alcoholic beverage 
application since it was asserted that Mr. Barrera had a 
conflict of interest within the meaning of Section 87100 et 
seq. of the Government Code. 
As I understand the proposed operation at the Walnut 
and Glassell site, J and L Oil Company proposes to sell 
gasoline which is to be dispensed strictly on a self-service 
basis. 
In order to pay for the gasoline, it will be necessary 
to exit from the vehicle, place the gasoline in the vehicle, 
enter the interior of the service station building and pay 
for the gasoline as well as any merchandise, including beer 
and wine, that the customer may wish to purchase while 
inside the premises. 
There is to be no automobile service 
or maintenance of any type at the site. 
Indeed, the former 
hydraulically operated vehicle lifts have been removed from 
the premises. 
Therefore, no garage activity will take place 
here. 
There will be air and water available for customers 
desiring same, but such air and water will not be located at 
the gasoline pump islands but at another point on the site. 
The Traffic Engineering Department has conducted 
traffic counts in the vicinity of Batavia Street and Chapman 
Avenue the location of Mr. Barrera's Union Oil Station and 
the intersection of Glassell and Walnut; the location of the 
J & L Oil Company site.

Basically, no one ever really assumed there was any 
conflict of interest involving Councilman Barrera's business 
operation and the proposed operation. 
After all, Councilman 
Barrera's operation is essentially a garage business with

ORDINANCE NO. 52-79 
AN INTERIM ORDINANCE OF THE CITY COUNCIL OF 
THE CITY OF ORANGE ADOPTED AS AN URGENCY MEASURE 
AMENDING SECTION 9l08(f) OF THE ORANGE MUNICIPAL 
CODE BY THE ADDITION OF A REQUIREMENT FOR A 
CONDITIONAL USE PERMIT FOR THE SALE OR DELIVERY 
OF ALCOHOLIC BEVERAGES AS AN ACCESSORY USE TO 
THE OPERATION OF AN AUTOMOBILE SERVICE STATION. 
WHEREAS, there is an application now before the Alcoholic Beverage Control 
for a 20 beer and wine sales license to be located at 480 North Glassell Street; 
and 
WHEREAS, if approved, said beer and wine sales would be made as an adjunct 
to gasoline and petroleum products sales; and 
WHEREAS, the Orange Police Department has reported this area to be one with 
serious law enforcement problems; and 
WHEREAS, the City Council has determined that there is presently a great 
concentration of liquor stores in said area; and 
WHEREAS, the City Council desires to protest the issue of said license; and 
WHEREAS, the City Council wants the matter of any application for the sale 
of liquor on the same premises as a service station to be subject to close 
review and a conditional use permit; and 
WHEREAS, no such conditional use permit shall be issued unless there shall 
be a fully-noticed public hearing and the applicant can comply with reasonable 
conditions imposed to protect the health, safety and welfare of the citizens of 
Orange. 
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF ORANGE DOES ORDAIN AS 
FOLLOWS: 
SECTION I: 
The following is hereby added to Section 9108(f) of the Orange Municipal 
Code, and shall read as follows: 
Section 9108(f) 
13. 
When the sale or delivery of any alcoholic beverage is proposed as an 
accessory use to the operation of an automobile service station. 
SECTION II: 
Yne reasons for the urgency of this ordinance are as follows: 
Because of problems of law enforcement, the City Council has determined 
that each application for the sale of liquor as an accessory use to the operation 
of a service station, shall be subject to a conditional use permit. 
The City 
Council desires that the application for such a license at 480 North Glassell 
Street should also be subject to the conditional use permit process, including a 
fully-noticed public hearing. 
An urgency ordinance is hereby enacted because a 
ordinance would 
at least thirty (30) days before it could become 
effective. 
Said application is now before Alcoholic Beverage Control. 
The City 
now has less than thirty (30) days in which to protest said application.

such time as a permanent ordinance can be enacted.

ZONING ADMINISTRATOR'S 
DECISION FOR 
CONDITIONAL USE PERMIT 1023 
Hearing Date: 
March 20, 1980 
3:00 P.M. 
Staff Present: John Lane, Zoning Administrator, Shirley George, Secretary 
Applicant & Owner: 
J & L Oil Company 
Location of Property: 
Subject property is located on the southwest corner of Glassell 
Street and Walnut Avenue, and addressed at 480 N. Glassell Street. 
REQUEST: 
To allow an off-sale beer and wine license in conjunction with 
a gasoline service station and convenience market. 
DETAILS OF THE HEARING: 
Mr. Lane, zoning Administrator, opened the public hearing stating 
that the applicant's are proposing to sell beer and wine in a 
convenience market along with the sale of gasoline. 
He indicated 
that the customers would leave their automobiles to purchase the 
beer and wine and pay for same inside the building. 
Mr. Lane stated 
that one of the requirements in filing the application is that the 
applicant's indicate by letter how they intend to collect the money. 
The applicant's have submitted such a letter which is a part of the 
file. 
Public hearing opened. 
Mr. v-Jilliam T. Garner, Attorney, 4220 Atlantic Avenue, Long Beach, 
spoke on behalf of his applicants, J & L Oil Company. 
Mr. Garner 
presented a petition in support of the application, which contains 
approximately 1,105 signatures. 
He indicated that 155 persons live 
within a few blocks of the proposed operation. 
Mr. Garner stated 
that he had read the staff's report and that there appears to be 
three reasons given why the staff is recommending denial of the 
application. 
Mr. Garner stated that Reasons one and two are 
similar and are difficult to distinguish, however, it appears 
to him that they are reasons for granting the application, rather 
than for denial. 
Mr. Garner indicated that his clients are family 
members, and this operation will not be a chain store or franchise 
type operation. 
He indicated that the convenience market will have 
groceries, dairy products and the beer and wine. 
He spoke of concern 
on the part of the city relative to traffic problems indicating that 
he does not see where a traffic problem exists, however, anyone going 
into business would hope to increase the business, therefore, traffic 
would increase also. 
Mr. Garner stated that Reason #3 is the only 
one in the staff's report that seems to possibly have any SUbstance. 
He indicated, however, that the selling of alcoholic beverages to 
elementary age students has not been a problem as obviously the 
owners would suffer badly for selling alcoholic beverages to minors.

FACTS 
The proposed premises are located at the southwest 
corner of Glassell and Walnut in the City of Orange (See Map 
Location No.2 & Photographs No. 2A & 2B). 
Formerly, this 
site was occupied by a Gulf Oil Station which ceased business 
operation several years ago. 
Several months ago, in August 
of 1979, the applicant, J and L Oil Company, first applied 
for a license to dispense beer and wine on an off-sale basis 
and conduct a convenience market dispensing of gasoline by 
self-service at the proposed location. No merchandise would 
be delivered to anyone in a vehicle and everyone would have 
to exit from the vehicle in order to supply his vehicle with 
gasoline and to purchase any merchandise, including beer and 
wine, within the interior of the building. 
The City Council 
authorized our office to transmit a protest to this application 
to the Alcoholic Beverage Control Department. 
This protest 
was based on the objection of the residents in the neighborhood 


May 5, 1980  
inasmuch as they felt that there were too many liquor stores 
and markets selling beer and wine in the vicinity. 
A copy 
of this letter of protest to the Alcoholic Beverage Control 
dated September 19, 1979, is enclosed herein. 
The minutes of the City Council of September 18, 1979, 
which evidence the decision of the Council to file the 
protest are also enclosed herein. 
At the conclusion of this 
protest hearing on September 18, 1979, the City Attorney was 
instructed to prepare an urgency ordinance pursuant to the 
authority of Section 65856 of the Government Code which 
urgency ordinance was thereupon adopted and became immediately 
effective on September 18, 1979 with its period of effectiveness 
extending to January 18, 1980. 
A copy of this Urgency 
Ordinance 52-79 is enclosed herein. 
Prior to the expiration 
of the urgency ordinance, the Planning staff was instructed 
to present to the Planning Commission a proposed amendment 
to the C-l Zone (which was the zone applicable to the proposed 
premises) amending the requirements of that zone insofar as 
the sale of alcoholic beverages at a service station site 
were concerned. 
The ultimate ordinance amending the C-l 
Zone provided that to sell alcoholic beverages on an off-
sale basis at a service station site required the approval 
of a Conditional Use Permit. 
Upon the enactment on February 26, 1980 of this permanent 
Ordinance No. 69-79, a copy of which is enclosed herein, the 
applicant, J and L Oil Company, filed an application for 
Conditional Use Permit No. 1023 to permit the off-sale sale 
of beer and wine in conjunction with a convenience market 
dispensing self service gasoline at the subject location. 
Pursuant to Ordinance No. 69-79, the Zoning Administrator 
was required to hear this application initially. 
On March 
20, 1980, Mr. John Lane, the Zoning Administrator, heard the 
Conditional Use Permit application in a public hearing and 
determined to grant the requested conditional use permit 
subject to the conditions listed in his decision. 
A copy of 
the Zoning Administrator's decision is enclosed herein. 
A number of residents in the area were opposed to the 
decision of the Zoning Administrator and prevailed upon 
Councilman Fred Barrera to appeal the granting of the 
conditional use permit. 
Councilman Barrera did appeal the 
granting of the conditional use permit on March 28, 1980. 
In accordance with Section 17.94.110 of the Orange Municipal 
Code (see attached) any interested party, member of the 
Planning Commission or City Council can appeal a decision of 
the Zoning Administrator. 
By virtue of the fact that the 
councilmen appealed the decision, the residents saved the 
$50 appeal fee normally required to appeal such decision. 
Of course, as you can see throughout the entire history 
of this proposed use, Councilman Barrera has participated in

the adoption of both the urgency ordinance, the permanent 
ordinance, and in the council discussion of September 18, 
1979 resulting in the filing of the protest with the Alcoholic 
Beverage Control all of which events occurred prior to the 
present hearing on appeal. 
In addition, of course, Mr. 
Barrera initiated the appeal of the Zoning Administrator's 
approval of this conditional use permit and was prepared to 
deliberate on this appeal at the time the matter was set 
before the Council on Tuesday, April 22, 1980. 
At the time 
of the Council hearing on April 22, 1980, the enclosed 
letter was received from the attorney for the applicant, J 
and L Oil Company, objecting to the fact that Mr. Barrera 
had in any manner participated in this alcoholic beverage 
application since it was asserted that Mr. Barrera had a 
conflict of interest within the meaning of Section 87100 et 
seq. of the Government Code. 
As I understand the proposed operation at the Walnut 
and Glassell site, J and L Oil Company proposes to sell 
gasoline which is to be dispensed strictly on a self-service 
basis. 
In order to pay for the gasoline, it will be necessary 
to exit from the vehicle, place the gasoline in the vehicle, 
enter the interior of the service station building and pay 
for the gasoline as well as any merchandise, including beer 
and wine, that the customer may wish to purchase while 
inside the premises. 
There is to be no automobile service 
or maintenance of any type at the site. 
Indeed, the former 
hydraulically operated vehicle lifts have been removed from 
the premises. 
Therefore, no garage activity will take place 
here. 
There will be air and water available for customers 
desiring same, but such air and water will not be located at 
the gasoline pump islands but at another point on the site. 
The Traffic Engineering Department has conducted 
traffic counts in the vicinity of Batavia Street and Chapman 
Avenue the location of Mr. Barrera's Union Oil Station and 
the intersection of Glassell and Walnut; the location of the 
J & L Oil Company site.

Basically, no one ever really assumed there was any 
conflict of interest involving Councilman Barrera's business 
operation and the proposed operation. 
After all, Councilman 
Barrera's operation is essentially a garage business with

the incidental sale of gasoline to service the community and 
assist the garage business whereas the proposed operation is 
essentially a convenience market with the sale of galoline 
as a leader to encourage the sale of items in the convenience 
market. 
Indeed, in a conversation on Thursday, April 24, 
1980, which I had with one of the sons of the applicant and 
apparently a partner in the business, the son describes the 
business as just that; primarily a convenience market providing 
grocery items and beer and wine with self-service gasoline 
sales as a leader to encourage customers to patronize the 
market. 
In fact, the proposed use seems very similar to the 
Fast Gas Self-Service station and convenience market selling 
beer and wine and located perhaps a mile or so west of 
Councilman Barrera's station on Chapman Avenue. 
There is no 
vehicle service provided at the Fast Gas Station either. 
Hours at Fast Gas are from 6:00 a.m. to 9:00 p.m. weekdays 
and 8:00 a.m. to 9:00 p.m. weekends. 
To say Councilman Barrera's station competes with the 
proposed business is almost like saying the Quick's Garage 
at Chapman and Flower competes with the Fast Gas station and 
convenience market immediately across the street from Quick's 
Garage. 
(see photograph No. 7 and No. 8 on the City Map) 
The only difference between Quick's and Fast Gas is that 
Quick's sells no gasoline. 
All pumps have been removed from 
the former service station site. 
The only common element in 
Councilman Barrera's operation and the proposed convenience 
market is the sale of small volumes of gasoline at both 
sites. 
Also, would we say Councilman Barrera's station 
competes with the 7-Eleven Market having a gasoline pump 
island located at Main Street and Palmyra Avenue? 
(see 
Photograph No. 8 and No. 9 on City Map) . 
Indeed, the applicant's son explained to me that, as an 
independent gasoline distributor, he was not sure they would 
always be able to obtain gasoline supplies. 
The purpose of 
the gasoline would be to act as a leader to get customers to 
enter the store and buy the merchandise located inside. 
Of course, I did not attempt to describe every gasoline 
station in the City of Orange. 
Suffice it to say there are 
both convenience market self-serve gasoline stations and full 
vehicle service stations east of Councilman Barrera's 
business on East Chapman near the Newport Freeway and also 
north of the proposed site on Glassell Street between the 
Riverside Freeway and the proposed site. 
I would appreciate your attention to this matter since 
the hearing before the Council was continued until May 20, 
1980, to permit me to contact your office for an opinion 


May 5, 1980  
concerning whether there is a conflict of interest in the 
matter described herein as to Mr. Fred Barrera, City Councilman. 
Thank you for your attention to this matter.

ORDINANCE NO. 52-79 
AN INTERIM ORDINANCE OF THE CITY COUNCIL OF 
THE CITY OF ORANGE ADOPTED AS AN URGENCY MEASURE 
AMENDING SECTION 9l08(f) OF THE ORANGE MUNICIPAL 
CODE BY THE ADDITION OF A REQUIREMENT FOR A 
CONDITIONAL USE PERMIT FOR THE SALE OR DELIVERY 
OF ALCOHOLIC BEVERAGES AS AN ACCESSORY USE TO 
THE OPERATION OF AN AUTOMOBILE SERVICE STATION. 
WHEREAS, there is an application now before the Alcoholic Beverage Control 
for a 20 beer and wine sales license to be located at 480 North Glassell Street; 
and 
WHEREAS, if approved, said beer and wine sales would be made as an adjunct 
to gasoline and petroleum products sales; and 
WHEREAS, the Orange Police Department has reported this area to be one with 
serious law enforcement problems; and 
WHEREAS, the City Council has determined that there is presently a great 
concentration of liquor stores in said area; and 
WHEREAS, the City Council desires to protest the issue of said license; and 
WHEREAS, the City Council wants the matter of any application for the sale 
of liquor on the same premises as a service station to be subject to close 
review and a conditional use permit; and 
WHEREAS, no such conditional use permit shall be issued unless there shall 
be a fully-noticed public hearing and the applicant can comply with reasonable 
conditions imposed to protect the health, safety and welfare of the citizens of 
Orange. 
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF ORANGE DOES ORDAIN AS 
FOLLOWS: 
SECTION I: 
The following is hereby added to Section 9108(f) of the Orange Municipal 
Code, and shall read as follows: 
Section 9108(f) 
13. 
When the sale or delivery of any alcoholic beverage is proposed as an 
accessory use to the operation of an automobile service station. 
SECTION II: 
Yne reasons for the urgency of this ordinance are as follows: 
Because of problems of law enforcement, the City Council has determined 
that each application for the sale of liquor as an accessory use to the operation 
of a service station, shall be subject to a conditional use permit. 
The City 
Council desires that the application for such a license at 480 North Glassell 
Street should also be subject to the conditional use permit process, including a 
fully-noticed public hearing. 
An urgency ordinance is hereby enacted because a 
ordinance would 
at least thirty (30) days before it could become 
effective. 
Said application is now before Alcoholic Beverage Control. 
The City 
now has less than thirty (30) days in which to protest said application. 


Ordinance No. 52-79  
THEREFORE, pursuant to the procedures of Section 36937 of the Government 
Code. the City Council of the City of Orange hereby enacts this ordinance as an 
urgency measure in order to protect the public health, safety and welfare until 
such time as a permanent ordinance can be enacted. 
ADOPTED this 18th day of September, 1979. 
ROBERT D. HOYT 
Mayor of the City of Orange

We are informed that the appellant, Councilman Fred L. Barrera, owns 
or operates a gasoline service station in close proximity to the property of 
our client .. J & L Oil Company. at 480 North Glassell Street, Orange, 
California. As you know, J & L Oil Company sells gasoline and operates 
a convenience market at this address. 
The appeal referred to above is from the March 20 .. 1980, decision of the 
Zoning Administrator for the City of Orange granting Conditional Use Permit 
1023 allowing the issuance to our client of an off-sale beer and wine license for 
use at said convenience market and the denial of such license would, of course .. 
be detrimental to J & L Oil Company's general business operation at that 
location. 
Under the circumstances, it is now clear to us that Mr. Barrera is a business 
competitor of our client and, therefore, that he has a financial interest in this 
appeal. It is noted that the filing of this appeal by Mr. Barrera, as well as 
his participation in any decision concerning it, is expressly prohibited by the 
provisions of Section 87100 of the Government Code of the State of California, 
a copy of which is attached hereto. It may be also that the ordinance itself

The following advice is provided pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b). The facts as I understand them from you and the City Planning Director are as follows. The J & L Oil Company (hereinafter referred to as J & L) operated a gasoline station in the City of Orange. In February of 1980 J & L filed an application for a conditional use permit to dispense beer and wine and to operate a convenience store at the station. In order to sell beer and wine, the station would be limited to self-service pumps. The application submitted by J & L was heard and approved by the Zoning Administrator March 20, 1980. Residents in the area who were opposed to the application asked Councilman Barrera to appeal the Administrator's decision to the City Council so they could avoid payment of a $50 appeals fee which would have been required if they had instituted the appeal themselves. Councilman Barrera did appeal the granting of the conditional use permit March 28, 1980, and the matter is now pending before the City Council. Councilman Barrera owns a gasoline station at the corner of Chapman and Batavia Streets, approximately one mile from the J & L station. Seventy-five percent of the gross income of Councilman Barrera's station results from his service and repair business, and the remaining 25 percent results from the sale of gasoline and oil products. His station pumps approximately 30,000 gallons of'<]asoline a month, but this figure varies by as much as 6 to 8 thousand gallons from month-to-month. Councilman Barrera's station is closed on Saturdays, Sundays and weekday evenings after 6:00 p.m. · ) Furman A. Roberts June 3, 1980 Page Two Councilman Barrera's station is located on Chapman Avenue, one of the city's major commercial streets, approximately one-half mile west of Glassell Street. The J & L station is located on the corner of Glassell and Walnut Avenue, approximately one-half mile north of Chapman and is not visible from Councilman Barrera's station. The Department of Energy has determined that for gasoline allocation purposes the market area for a service station in an urban area (population exceeds 25,000) is a radius of one mile from the station. There are approximately eight stations, including the J & L station, located in Councilman Barrera's market area. embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter regarding whether Councilman Fred Barrera must disqualify himself from voting on J & L Oil Company's conditional use permit application to sell beer and wine at a gas station located approximately one mile from his own competing station. The FPPC concluded that disqualification is not required because competitive effects would be spread among multiple businesses in the area and no material financial effect on Barrera's station is reasonably foreseeable.
